items.question: Explain agglomerative hierarchical clustering in plain language.
items.answer: Start with each point as its own cluster. Repeatedly measure distances between clusters (via a linkage rule), merge the two closest, and record each merge in a dendrogram. Stop when one cluster remains; cut the tree later to get any desired number of groups.

items.question: What is the difference between single, complete, and Ward linkage?
items.answer: Single uses the minimum point-to-point distance across clusters (chaining risk). Complete uses the maximum (compact clusters). Ward minimizes the increase in pooled within-cluster variance when merging, assuming Euclidean geometry—often good for numeric blobs.

items.question: How do you choose the number of clusters from a dendrogram?
items.answer: Inspect merge heights for large vertical gaps indicating dissimilar groups joining late; cut at that height. Complement visual judgment with metrics (silhouette, cophenetic correlation) and business constraints on minimum cluster size.

items.question: Why is hierarchical clustering slow on large n?
items.answer: Naive agglomerative methods maintain and update an n×n distance structure; time is often O(n³) and memory O(n²). For millions of points, subsample, use approximate methods, or switch to k-means/DBSCAN for initial partitioning.
